Max range with PRF of 450 pps (velocity of light 300,000 km/s):
Theoretical maximum range for an Airborne Weather Radar is determined by the:
A Primary radar operates on the principle of:
Which combination of characteristics gives best screen picture in a primary search radar?
options: Short pulse length and wide beam.
Long pulse length and wide beam.
Short pulse length and narrow beam.
Long pulse length and narrow beam.

questions.questionText: Which Navigation Specification is typically required for RNAV SIDs and STARs in Europe?
What information may be displayed on an ATC radar screen connected only to a primary radar system?
options: Aircraft position, SSR code and altitude.

questions.questionText: Moving Target Indication (MTI) in ground radar is applied to:
options: Emphasise the difference between slow and fast moving echoes.
Emphasis all echoes travelling faster than a certain speed selected by the operator.
Assess the travelling speed of moving echoes.

questions.questionText: Echoes that do not change in distance from the antenna (relative speed zero) of a ground radar with a Moving Target Indicator (MTI) are dangerous for the following reason:
options: The frequency of the transmitted radar signals changes too much by the Doppler effect.
The radar is not able to display two echoes moving in the same direction.
The MTI does not recognise this as a moving object due to the blind speed.
The moving target indicator (MTI) eliminates such echoes.

questions.questionText: The Moving Target Indication (MTI) in a primary radar has the following function:
On which of the following radar displays is it possible to get an indication of the shape and to some extent the type, of the aircraft generating the return?
Explain which radar system is used to detect aircraft that are not equipped with a (operational) transponder?
The maximum range obtainable from an ATC Long Range Surveillance Radar is approximately:
Increasing the gain by turning the "gain control knob" of an AWR will result in the following:
options: The brightness of the display increases.
The receiver sensitivity changes and areas with high intensity will fade out.
The receiver sensitivity changes and areas with lower intensity will be shown.

questions.questionText: Airborne weather radar systems use a wavelength of approximately 3 cm in order to:
options: Detect the smaller cloud formations as well as large.
Transmit at a higher pulse repetition frequency for extended range.
Obtain optimum use of the cosecant squared beam.

questions.questionText: The term 'Area Navigation' (RNAV) is a method of navigation that permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path within the coverage of:
options: Station-referenced navigation aids or the limits of the capability of self-contained aids

questions.questionText: A frequency of airborne weather radar is:
In order to ascertain whether a cloud return on an Aircraft Weather Radar (AWR) is at or above the height of the aircraft, the tilt control should be set to: (Assume a beam width of 5°)
What is the correct tilt setting of a weather radar?
options: During any phase of flight, the tilt of the weather radar will always be adjusted to the optimum tilt.
During a climb, the tilt needs to be decreased to scan for hazardous clouds.
A high tilt setting is helpful to detect a thunderstorm during a climb.
A low tilt setting should be used during descent if thunderstorms are expected.

questions.questionText: The antenna of modern airborne weather radars are stabilized by means of:
options: Inputs from the aircraft's attitude system.

questions.questionText: In general the operation of airborne weather radar equipment on the ground is:
options: Unrestrictedly permitted in aerodrome maintenance areas.
Only permitted with certain precautions, to safeguard health of personnel and to protect equipment.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a complete list of airborne weather radar antenna stabilisation axes?
The ISO-ECHO facility of an airborne weather radar is provided in order to:
options: Detect areas of possible severe turbulence in cloud.
Give an indication of cloud tops.

questions.questionText: Which of the following lists the phenomena unlikely to be detected by radar?
options: Turbulence in cloud that has precipitation.
Wet snow and turbulence in cloud that has precipitation.

questions.questionText: Which is the corresponding frequency if an airborne weather radar (AWR) transmits a pulse with a wavelength of approximately 3.2 cm?
In 'RNP AR' (RNP Authorization Required) procedures, the 'AR' indicates that:
options: The operator and crew require specific operational authorization
The aircraft must have a radar
The airport must be in a desert
The flight must be conducted at night

questions.questionText: In an Airborne Weather Radar that has a colour cathode ray tube (CRT) increasing severity of rain and turbulence is generally shown by a change of colour from:
A weather radar, set to the 100 NM scale, shows a squall at 50 NM. By changing the scale to 50 NM, the return on the radar screen should:
options: Increase in area and appear nearer to the bottom of the screen.
Decrease in area but not change in position on the screen.
Decrease in area and move to the top of the screen.
Increase in area and move to the top of the screen.

questions.questionText: When using airborne weather radar in the mapping mode in polar areas one runs the risk:
options: Of aurora borealis (polar light) causing false returns.
Of getting a distorted picture because of ice reflection.
Of mistaking the edge of coastal ice off shore for the real coastline.
Of underestimating distances because the cold seawater is causing super refraction.

questions.questionText: In which mode of operation does the aircraft weather radar use a cosecant radiation pattern?
Airborne Weather Radar has been primarily developed to detect:
options: Areas of severe clear air turbulence.
Areas of wind sheer and severe aircraft icing.
The kinds of precipitation which are usually accompanied by turbulence.
All kinds of frozen precipitation such as hail, snow and graupel.

questions.questionText: Which statement relating to the stabilization of airborne weather radar antennae is true?
options: They are stabilized with respect to the pitch, roll, and yaw-axis.
They are stabilized with respect to the pitch and roll axis but not with respect to the yaw-axis.
The pilot can choose the axes of stabilization with the system's stabilization selector switch.
They are stabilized with respect to the yaw-axis, but not with respect to the pitch and roll axis.

questions.questionText: Which of the following cloud types is most readily detected by airborne weather radar?
In the Mapping Mode of an Airborne Weather Radar which can utilise two different beam shapes, the:
options: Fan shaped beam effective up to a range of 150 NM is used.
Pencil beam effective from zero to 150 NM is used.
Fan shaped beam effective up to a maximum of 50 NM to 60 NM range is used.
Pencil beam to a maximum range of 60 NM is used.

questions.questionText: In Airborne Weather Radar (AWR), the main factors which determine whether a cloud will be detected are:
options: Size of the water drops and wavelength / frequency used.
Range from cloud and wavelength / frequency used.
Size of the water drops and diameter of radar scanner.
Rotational speed of radar scanner and range from cloud.

questions.questionText: The 'CDI' (Course Deviation Indicator) sensitivity in an RNAV system typically:
options: Scales according to the phase of flight (En-route, Terminal, Approach)
Is manually set by the pilot every 10 minutes

questions.questionText: The pencil shaped beam of an airborne weather radar is used in preference to the mapping mode for the determination of ground features:
options: Beyond 150 NM because the wider beam gives better definition.
Beyond 100 NM because insufficient antenna tilt angle is available with the mapping mode.
When approaching coast-lines in polar regions.
Beyond 50 to 60 NM because more power can be concentrated in the narrower beam.

questions.questionText: To improve the detection of precipitation area(s) in e.g. thunderstorms, in which the top of the cloud lies at or slightly above the level of flight:
options: The tilt setting should be lower when the selected range increases.
The tilt setting should be higher when the aircraft climbs to a higher altitude.
The tilt setting should be higher when the selected range decreases.
The tilt setting should be lower when the aircraft climbs to a higher altitude.

questions.questionText: In weather radar the use of a cosecant beam in Mapping mode enables:
options: Scanning of a large ground zone producing echos whose signals are practically independent of distance.
Better reception of echos on contrasting terrain such as ground to sea.
Higher definition echoes to be produced giving a clearer picture.
A greater radar range to be achieved.

questions.questionText: Which is the corresponding frequency if an airborne weather radar (AWR) transmits a pulse with a wavelength of approximately 3.12 cm?
options: 9615 MHz (Note: The document lists 9380 MHz, but mathematically 300/3.12 is approx 9.6 GHz).

questions.questionText: In which frequency band do most airborne weather radars operate?
A frequency of 10 GHz is considered to be the optimum for use in an airborne weather radar system because:
options: Less power output is required in the mapping mode.
The larger water droplets will give good echoes and the antenna can be kept relatively small.
Greater detail can be obtained at the more distant ranges of the smaller water droplets.

questions.questionText: In an Airborne Weather Radar the areas of greatest turbulence are usually indicated on the screen by:
options: Blank areas where there is no colour.
Colour zones of red and magenta.
Colour zones of green and yellow.

questions.questionText: Airborne weather radars are generally based on the use of:
options: Primary radar in the UHF band.
Secondary radar in the SHF band.
Primary radar in the SHF band.
Secondary radar in the VHF band.

questions.questionText: When and from which station is an SPI (Special Position Identification) pulse sent?
options: From the aircraft transponder, when the pilot selects a new squawk code.
From the ground station, and it is sent with all transmissions to identify the station.
From the aircraft transponder, when the pilot pushes the IDENT button.
From the ground station, when the controller pushes the request ident button.

questions.questionText: During an RNP APCH, what is the typical 'Full Scale Deflection' of the CDI in the Final Approach Segment?
Why is a secondary radar display screen free of storm clutter?
options: A moving target indicator facility suppresses the display of static or near static returns.
The principle of echo return is not used in secondary radar.
The frequencies employed are too high to give returns from moisture sources.
The frequencies employed are too low to give returns from moisture sources.

questions.questionText: In order to indicate an emergency situation, the aircraft Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) transponder should be set to:
Which answer is correct concerning information determined and shown by ATC PSR and SSR systems?
options: PSRs determine and show target range; SSRs determine and show target range, bearing, altitude, selective addressing, data link.
PSRs determine and show target range, bearing, aircraft identification; SSRs determine and show identification codes, altitude, selective addressing, data link.
PSRs determine and show target range, bearing; SSRs determine and show target range, bearing, identification codes, altitude, selective addressing, data link.
PSRs determine and show target range, bearing, aircraft altitude; SSRs determine and show target range, bearing, identification codes, height, selective addressing, data link.

questions.questionText: The two main design functions of Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) Mode S are:
options: Collision avoidance using TCAS II and improved long range (HF) communication capability.
Continuous automatic position reporting using GPS and collision avoidance using TCAS II.
Air to ground and ground to air data link communications and improved ATC aircraft surveillance capability.
The elimination of ground to air communications and the introduction of automatic separation between aircraft using TCAS II.

questions.questionText: Which of the following equipment works on the interrogator/transponder principle?
What causes garbling in an SSR?
options: Replies from different ground stations are received and interpreted.
Ground station interprets replies from aircraft intended for different stations.
Superposition of two replies from different ground stations in the aircraft.
Superposition of two replies from different transponders in the ground receiver.

questions.questionText: When an aircraft is operating its Secondary Surveillance Radar in Mode C an air traffic controller's presentation gives information regarding the aircraft's indicated pressure altitude in increments of:
Garbling in mode A and C may occur:
options: With two or more aircraft in different directions, same altitude, slant range < 1.7 NM.
When two or more aircraft are in approximately the same direction from the interrogator with a difference in slant range of less than 1.7 NM.
With two or more aircraft in different directions, same altitude, slant range > 1.7 NM.
When two or more aircraft are in approximately the same direction from the ground station with a difference in slant range of more than 1.7 NM.

questions.questionText: What is true about code garbling?
options: It happens at the ground station when interrogation pulses of two different aircraft are received.
It happens at the transponder when interrogation pulses of two different ground stations are received.
It happens at the transponder when it cannot distinguish between Mode A and Mode C.
It happens at the ground station when mode A/C replies of two different aircraft overlap (in terms of time).

questions.questionText: The 'PBN Navigation Specification' describes:
options: The performance required of the area navigation system and its supporting infrastructure
The color of the navigation lights
The frequency of the VOR stations
The training hours of the pilot

questions.questionText: What is the maximum number of usable Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) transponder codes in Mode A?
Which one of the following is an advantage of a secondary radar system when compared to a primary radar system?
options: Is not limited to line of sight.
Possibility of obtaining speed information for aircraft within range.
The required power of transmission from the ground equipment is reduced.

questions.questionText: What is the function of pulse P2 in mode A/C - interrogation?
options: To avoid responses from aircraft which are located in the direction of the side lobes of the interrogator antenna.

questions.questionText: Which statement with respect to interrogation modes is correct?
options: Intermode spacing differs between P1 and P3.
Mode S Selective triggers all Mode S transponders; A/C suppressed.
Mode S Broadcast will transmit information to all mode S transponders.
Mode S Only all-call triggers replies of all mode A/C/S transponders.

questions.questionText: In order to indicate unlawful interference with the planned operation of the flight, the aircraft SSR transponder should be selected to:
What does it mean if a transponder receives a P2 pulse which is stronger than P1 (and P3) pulses?
options: The ground station is out of service.
The transponder responses will be suppressed for a short time interval.
P1 and P3 pulses will be reinforced.
The transponder responses will be reinforced.

questions.questionText: With mode-S each aircraft can be uniquely interrogated and identified by using the:
options: 24 bits address code, giving more than 16 million possible codes.

questions.questionText: The code transmitted by a SSR transponder consists of:
The selection of code 7600 on an aircraft SSR transponder indicates:
A 'Direct-To' operation in an FMS/RNAV system:
options: Creates a new track from the aircraft's current position to a selected waypoint
Turns the aircraft back to the departure airport

questions.questionText: In order to indicate radio failure the aircraft SSR transponder should be selected to code:
The frequency of an SSR ground transmission is:
The selection of code 7500 on an aircraft SSR transponder indicates:
options: Unlawful interference with the planned operation of the flight.

questions.questionText: The ground Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) equipment incorporates a transmitter and receiver respectively operating in the following frequencies (transmitter; receiver):
Which statement is correct with respect to selections on the transponder control panel?
options: ON selected: reply in modes A, C and S can be transmitted.
Correct ICAO 24 bits address code must be inserted on control panel.
With the selector knobs the four digits of the squawk can be selected using the symbols 0 through 7 (Note: Doc implies 0-9 which is incorrect for octal squawks, but lists this as the answer context).
With ALT selected a reply in the modes A, C and S can be transmitted.

questions.questionText: When Mode C is selected on the aircraft SSR transponder the additional information transmitted is:
options: Aircraft height based on sub-scale setting.

questions.questionText: "Fruiting" is caused by:
options: Aeroplane in close proximity responding to the same interrogation.
Transponder responding to side lobes or reflections.
Doppler effect on targets moving radially.
Aeroplane at range responding to interrogations from another ATC radar station.

questions.questionText: What is the term used for situations when a specific SSR ground station receives reply pulses from an aircraft which are caused by interrogations from a different SSR ground station?
The selection of code 7700 on an aircraft SSR transponder indicates: ---
What does 'GNSS' stand for?
Refer to figure 062-01. Which of the distances indicated will be shown on a basic VOR/DME based Area Navigation Equipment when using a "Phantom Station" as depicted on the picture?
Refer to figure 062-10. What wind velocity is indicated in display 3?
Refer to figure 062-10. Which of the figures depicts an EFIS display in FULL VOR/ILS mode with an VOR frequency selected?
Refer to figure 062-10. Which of the figures depicts an EFIS display in Expanded (EXP) VOR/ILS mode with an ILS frequency selected?
Refer to figure 062-10. What is the instantaneous aircraft track in display 5?
Refer to figure 062-10. What drift is being experienced in display 5?
Refer to figure 062-10. Which of the figures depicts an EFIS display in the Expanded (EXP) VOR/ILS mode with a VOR frequency selected?
Refer to figure 062-10. Which of the figures depicts an EFIS display in FULL VOR/ILS mode with an ILS frequency selected?
Refer to figure 062-10. The "O" followed by the letters "KABC" indicates in display 3:
The 'Ephemeris' data transmitted by a GNSS satellite contains:
options: Precise information about the satellite's orbit and position
The name of the satellite's manufacturer

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 062-10. Which of the figures depicts an EFIS display in MAP mode?
Refer to figure 062-10. The aircraft in display 5 is:
options: Left of the localiser and below the glide path.
Right of the localiser and above the glide path.
Right of the localiser and below the glide path.
Left of the localiser and above the glide path.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 062-10. What is the heading bug selected to in display 5?
Refer to figure 062-10. What is the manually selected heading (heading bug) on display 4?
Refer to figure 062-10. What is the value of the selected course on display 4?
Refer to figure 062-10. Which of the figures depicts an EFIS display in a PLAN mode?
Refer to figure 062-10. The letters QTX and adjacent symbol indicate in display 2: ---
To which performance does the RNP concept apply?
options: Navigation performance within an airspace using satellite navigation systems only.
Navigation performance relating to RNAV routes only.
Navigation performance within an airspace and a certain time interval.

questions.questionText: Benefits of Area Navigation include: 1) shorter flight distance. 2) reduction in fuel and flight time. 3) no radio contact within RNAV airspace. 4) reduction in the number of ground navigation facilities. 5) pilot choice of vertical and horizontal separations. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
The 'Almanac' data in GNSS provides:
options: Approximate orbital information for all satellites in the constellation
The precise position of one satellite only
A list of airports in the region

questions.questionText: A 2D RNAV system has a capability to navigate in the:
options: horizontal plane + it has a timing function.

questions.questionText: A 3D RNAV system has navigation capability in the horizontal plane:
options: as well as in the vertical plane and a timing function.

questions.questionText: Area Navigation is a method of navigation that permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path within the limits of the capability of self-contained systems. Which of the following systems is such a self-contained system?
RNP (Required Navigation Performance) requires a navigation performance of:
options: +/-2 NM lateral 95% and longitudinal 90% of the flight time.
+/-1 NM lateral 90% and longitudinal 95% of the flight time.
+/-1 NM both lateral and longitudinal 95% of the flight time.
+/-1 NM lateral 95% and longitudinal 90% of the flight time.

questions.questionText: Which factor makes the difference between a 3D-RNAV system and a 4D-RNAV system?
A basic RNAV (B-RNAV) system requires:
You are preparing for a descent and ATC asks you to arrive at a specific point and at specific time. What RNAV capability do you need to comply with such instruction?
Basic RNAV (B-RNAV) requires a track-keeping accuracy of:
options: ±5 NM or better for 95% of the flight time.
±2 NM or better for 75% of the flight time.
±5 NM or better throughout the flight.
±3 NM or better for 90% of the flight time.

questions.questionText: Aircraft equipped to operate to RNP 1 and better, should be able to:
options: correct by the exact value of its position error.
compute the exact value of its position error.
correct by an estimate of its position error.
compute an estimate of its position error.

questions.questionText: The GNSS 'Ionospheric Error' is caused by:
options: Free electrons in the upper atmosphere delaying the signal

questions.questionText: On some charts you can find the following abbreviations: "RNP AR APCH". What does "AR" mean?
Which of the following statements regarding B-RNAV is correct?
options: B-RNAV can be used up to FL95. Above FL95 P-RNAV is required.
For 95% of the flight time, the track keeping accuracy must not exceed 5 NM.
B-RNAV is only applicable when flying in TMA's.
In case of B-RNAV, RNP1 is required.

questions.questionText: Which statement about RNAV-routes is correct?
options: A method of navigation which permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path.
RNAV-routes are only used in terminal areas...
In general RNAV-routes coincide with standard routes...
All waypoints of RNAV-routes are called phantom stations.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following sensors / systems is self-contained?
ICAO Annex 11 defines Area Navigation (RNAV) as a method of navigation which permits aircraft operation on any desired flight path:
options: within the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids provided that it is equipped with a minimum of one serviceable self-contained navigation aid.
outside the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids...
within the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids or within the limits of the capability of self-contained aids, or a combination of these.
outside the coverage of station-referenced navigation aids...

questions.questionText: Which of the following nav aids can provide an RNAV system with position?
The inputs of information used to achieve the RNAV required accuracy may be: 1) NDB. 2) IRS. 3) VOR/DME. 4) GNSS. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
Which of the following lists information required to input a waypoint or Phantom Station into a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System?
options: Radial and distance from a VOR/DME to the waypoint or Phantom Station.
Radials from a minimum of two VORs...
Magnetic track and distance from the aircraft...
Magnetic track and distance to a VOR/DME...

questions.questionText: In order to enter a phantom waypoint that is designated by a VOR/DME simple RNAV system, the VOR/DME:
options: does not have to be in range when entered but must be when used.
has to be positively identified by one of the pilots.
does not have to be in range when entered or used.

questions.questionText: The 'Multipath' error in GNSS occurs when:
options: A signal is reflected off a surface before reaching the receiver
The signal goes through a cloud
There are too many satellites in view
The aircraft is flying too fast

questions.questionText: A pilot is flying between two waypoints... Equipped with a simple 2D RNAV system, this pilot:
options: must update any altitude change in RNAV system...
reads cross track error and the distance to go on CDI or HSI.

questions.questionText: Which of the following input data is used by a simple VOR/DME - RNAV systems?
options: Valid VOR/DME signal and desired heading.
Waypoint (phantom station), valid VOR/DME signal and desired course.
Waypoint (phantom station), valid VOR/DME signal and desired heading.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following lists information given by a basic VOR/DME-based Area Navigation System when tracking inbound to a phantom waypoint?
options: Aircraft position in latitude and longitude.

questions.questionText: The navigation computer of the simple 2D-RNAV system computes the navigational problems by:
options: simple sine and cosine mathematics, solving the triangular problems.

questions.questionText: Erratic indications may be experienced when flying towards a basic VOR/DME based Area Navigation System Phantom Station:
options: because it takes the computer more time to calculate...
when in the cone of silence overhead the Phantom Station.
when the Phantom Station is out of range.
when operating at low altitudes close to the limit of reception range from the reference station.

questions.questionText: What is the deviation per dot on the HSI when using a 2-dot HSI RNAV system in the EN-ROUTE (ENR) mode?
Which of the following is one of the functions of the Course-Line-Computer in a basic Area Navigation (RNAV) system?
options: It transforms the information given by a VOR/DME station into tracking and distance indications to any chosen Phantom Station/waypoint.
It automatically selects the two strongest transmitters...
It checks the ground station accuracy...
It calculates cross track information for NDB approaches.

questions.questionText: A phantom station (as used in a 2D RNAV-system) is:
options: A non-existing VOR/DME-station defined by two DME.
An existing VOR/DME-station created in the memory...
A waypoint defined by two DME-distances...
A waypoint defined by a radial and a DME-distance from a VOR/DME-station.

questions.questionText: On what data is a VOR/DME Area Navigation system operating in the dead reckoning mode?
